1.synthesis: Paul grounds the appeal to reconciliation that closed chapter 5 in an urgent warning: do not receive the grace of God in vain (v.1). The grace that accomplished reconciliation must not lie inert. He reinforces this with Isaiah's Servant oracle — 'In an acceptable time I have heard you, and in the day of salvation I have helped you' — and declares: behold, now is the acceptable time; now is the day of salvation (v.2). The apostolic ministry is itself the continuation of that Servant vocation: to co-labor with God in the application of what Christ accomplished. The long hardship catalogue that follows (vv.4–10) — afflictions, necessities, distresses, stripes, imprisonments, tumults, labors, watchings, fastings — is not a complaint but a credential: these are the marks that authenticate Paul's ministry as genuinely apostolic, precisely because they replicate the pattern of the suffering Servant. The paradoxical climax — as sorrowful yet always rejoicing; as poor yet making many rich; as having nothing yet possessing all things — reveals the hidden economy of apostolic labor.
1.historical_context: The citation of Isaiah 49:8 from the Second Servant Song situates Paul within the prophetic tradition of the Servant who co-labors with God in the restoration of Israel and the nations. The Servant is called to be a light to the nations, to open blind eyes and release captives — a vocation Paul understands himself to inherit in the apostolic mission (Acts 13:47). The hardship catalogue of vv.4–10 belongs to a recognizable form in both Jewish and Greco-Roman moral literature: the list of trials that test and prove character. Stoic philosophers compiled similar catalogues to demonstrate the sage's indifference to fortune; Paul transforms the form by embedding it in cruciform theology — the hardships are not overcome by inner detachment but are the very vehicle of divine power (4:7–12). The 'acceptable time' language also has eschatological weight in Jewish usage: the et ratzon (time of favor) was the appointed moment of divine intervention in history.
1.literary_structure: The paragraph opens with the negative condition (do not receive grace in vain) grounded in the positive eschatological announcement (now is the day of salvation). The appeal then modulates into self-defense: Paul narrates the apostolic life in a sustained rhetorical catalogue. The hardship list (vv.4–5) is followed by a virtue list (vv.6–7), both governed by 'as ministers of God.' The final set of paradoxes (vv.8–10) employs the Greek antithetical construction hōs … kai (as … yet also) to hold apparent opposites in tension: dying and behold we live; chastened and not killed; sorrowful yet always rejoicing. This is not rhetorical flourish but theological precision — the cruciform life produces exactly these simultaneous conditions.
1.theological_application: Chrysostom seizes on the phrase 'receiving not the grace of God in vain' as Paul's deliberate move to alarm those who, hearing that God beseeches them, might become complacent — 'lest they become supine.' The fact that God makes appeals through ambassadors is not a reason for ease but the very thing that makes urgency necessary: 'for this very reason let us make haste to please God and to collect spiritual merchandise.' The grace that reconciles is simultaneously a grace that constrains and commissions. Chrysostom's reading integrates chapter 5 and 6 as a single movement: the logic of reconciliation demands that those reconciled become laborers in the ministry of reconciliation. Gill on v.2 traces the Isaiah background carefully: God heard Christ in his earthly ministry 'when he put up that prayer to him' for the glorification of himself and the preservation and conversion of those given him — so 'now is the accepted time' announces that the moment of that divine hearing has arrived in the proclamation of the gospel. The day of salvation is not a future appointment but the present moment in which the apostolic word is received.
1.christology: The Servant background of Isaiah 49:8 connects to the Servant Christology Paul has been developing: Christ is the Servant heard by the Father in his suffering and exaltation; the 'acceptable time' is the time inaugurated by his resurrection. Paul's apostolic co-laboring 'with him' (v.1) means working with the risen Christ who is himself the active agent of the ongoing Servant mission. The hardship catalogue is therefore not evidence that Christ has abandoned his ministers but that Christ's own pattern — suffering leading to glory — is being reproduced in those who belong to him. The 'dying and behold we live' of v.9 is the resurrection pattern manifested in apostolic existence: 'always carrying in the body the dying of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may also be manifested' (4:10).

11.synthesis: Paul turns from credential-catalogue to direct address: 'Our mouth is open to you, O Corinthians; our heart is wide' (v.11). The restraint has been on their side, not his: 'You are not restricted by us, but you are restricted in your own affections' (v.12). He appeals for reciprocal enlargement of heart: 'In return — I speak as to children — widen your hearts also' (v.13). The brief section is an affectionate interruption of the argument, the father appealing to children who have withheld themselves. It models the relational dimension of apostolic ministry: the goal is not merely doctrinal compliance but open, unguarded fellowship.
11.historical_context: Chrysostom observes that Paul's detailed account of his hardships and the antithetical paradoxes in vv.4–10 was designed precisely to pry open the Corinthians' narrowed hearts — by showing them the full scope of apostolic affection expressed through suffering. The frank address 'O Corinthians' — the only place Paul uses their ethnic name directly in this letter — signals that what follows is deeply personal. The category of speaking 'as to children' (v.13) invokes the father-child metaphor Paul used in 1 Corinthians 4:14–15: Paul is their spiritual father; the appeal is familial, not merely managerial.
11.literary_structure: The section functions as a hinge in the chapter. The long self-defense of vv.1–10 concludes, and Paul breaks into direct personal appeal before pivoting to the covenantal warning of vv.14–18. The three verses exhibit a tight symmetry: open mouth and wide heart (Paul's openness) → restricted affections (their narrowness) → appeal to reciprocal widening. Gill notes that 'you are not straitened in us' means Paul and his co-workers have not caused the Corinthians distress or filled them with anguish; the narrowness is entirely on the Corinthian side — they have withheld themselves, not been repelled by apostolic coldness.
11.theological_application: Chrysostom, having traced the entire hardship catalogue as Paul's strategy for opening their hearts, identifies the pay-off of the argument: 'having detailed his own trials and afflictions … and having shown that the thing was a great good … and having called those things armor … he then makes his exhortation.' The argument has been laid; the appeal now comes. The pattern is instructive for pastoral ministry: Paul did not demand affection; he displayed it comprehensively and then invited reciprocation. The image of the wide heart (platynō, enlarge) is the positive counterpart to the metaphor of straitening: Christian fellowship at its deepest is characterized by expansion, not contraction — and the expansion must be mutual. Paul's willingness to speak with parental frankness rather than apostolic authority signals that what he seeks is genuine relationship, not mere deference.
11.christology: The open mouth and wide heart that Paul claims for himself are modeled on the ministry of Christ, who was himself not restricted in his affection for his people. The widening of hearts that Paul seeks from the Corinthians is ultimately a participation in the generosity that characterizes God's own love shed abroad by the Spirit (Romans 5:5). The father-child language simultaneously points forward and backward: Paul is their father in Christ (1 Corinthians 4:15), and God is the Father whose fatherly affection grounds all genuine human affection (Ephesians 3:14–15). The section thus anticipates the promise of v.18: 'I will be a Father to you' — the relational openness Paul seeks from the Corinthians is the created reflection of the divine fatherly openness declared in the covenant.

14.synthesis: Paul issues a covenantal call to separation: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (v.14). The call unfolds through a cascade of rhetorical questions — what partnership has righteousness with lawlessness? What fellowship has light with darkness? What accord has Christ with Belial? What portion has a believer with an unbeliever? What agreement has the temple of God with idols? (vv.14–16). The implied answer to each question is: none at all. The foundation is covenantal identity: 'We are the temple of the living God; as God said, I will dwell in them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they shall be my people' (v.16). Therefore: come out from among them and be separate, says the Lord; touch no unclean thing; and I will receive you, and will be a Father to you, and you shall be sons and daughters to me (vv.17–18).
14.historical_context: Gill identifies the 'unequally yoked' image as an allusion to the Mosaic prohibition of yoking an ox and a donkey together (Deuteronomy 22:10) — a visual picture of incompatible partnership in the same harness. In Corinth the prohibition was concrete: commercial guild meetings were held in temple precincts; civic festivals involved sacrificial meals; business partnerships were consecrated by oaths to city deities. The Jewish community maintained separation through Torah-observance; Gentile believers faced the same challenge without that legal scaffolding. Gill is careful to note that the command does not forbid civil association with unbelievers — such separation 'is impracticable' and 'many natural and civil relations subsisting among men make it absolutely necessary' — but it forbids covenant partnership and religious integration. The 'Belial' of v.15 is a Jewish name for the chief adversary, prominent in the Dead Sea Scrolls' War Scroll, representing the organizing power of darkness over against Christ.
14.literary_structure: The passage is structured as a chain of rhetorical questions (vv.14–16) followed by a chain of scriptural citations (vv.16–18). The rhetorical questions draw on antithetical pairs — righteousness/lawlessness, light/darkness, Christ/Belial, believer/unbeliever, temple/idols — each driving home the same point of incompatibility. The scriptural chain is a composite of Leviticus 26:11–12, Ezekiel 37:27 (the 'I will dwell and walk among them' promise), Isaiah 52:11 (the call to come out), and 2 Samuel 7:14 (the Father-son promise to David, now generalized to all sons and daughters). Gill notes that the call to 'come out from among them' was originally directed to the priests bearing the temple vessels out of Babylon — now applied to the whole people of God who are made priests in Christ.
14.theological_application: Gill on the 'temple of God with idols' image (v.16) draws the arresting old-covenant parallel: 'no more than the ark of the Lord had with Dagon, or Dagon with the ark; which when brought into his temple, and set by him, the idol fell down, and part of him was broke to pieces.' Proximity between the holy and the unholy does not sanctify the unholy but disgraces the holy. On the promise of v.18 — 'I will be a Father to you' — Gill is careful about tense and force: this 'is not to be understood of the first commencement of this relation, as though God now began to be their Father; nor indeed of the first manifestation of it, which had been already made to their souls by the Spirit of adoption … but of his acting, and continuing to act the part of a father to them.' The promise is not about entering the relationship but about the Father's ongoing active fathering — pitying, sympathizing, supporting, taking care. The call to separation is therefore not primarily legal prohibition but covenantal invitation: come out so that you may inhabit the fullness of what the Father promises to do among those who are his.
14.christology: The Christ-Belial antithesis (v.15) presents Christ as the organizing center of the new creation, standing over against the adversary who organized the old order of darkness. The Davidic Father-son promise of 2 Samuel 7:14 — originally concerning the Messiah — is now extended to 'sons and daughters' who are in the Messiah. This is the logic of union with Christ: the privileges promised to the Son are distributed to those who are joined to him. The temple imagery — 'we are the temple of the living God' (v.16) — points to Christ's own body as the true temple (John 2:19–21), and the church's temple-identity derives from her union with him: as he is the dwelling-place of God, so the community of those in him becomes the dwelling-place of God by the Spirit.
